content_text:
Thank you to everyone who joined us on Monday for our panel on how research informs public health and policy!

We're especially grateful to our panelists, Simbo Ige MD, MS, MPH (Chicago Department of Public Health), Matthew R. Smith (Illinois Department of Public Health (IDPH)), and Emma Adam—and to our moderator Thomas McDade, for an honest and energizing discussion about what it really takes to move research into policy.

A few key takeaways:

Closing the gap between research and policy takes real engagement. It starts with showing up to talk with legislators and charting ways to be part of decisions as they're being made.

Data aren't enough on their own. We need to listen ever more closely to communities and share research in clear, relatable ways that resonate.

We're grateful to our speakers for challenging us to communicate more clearly, listen more deeply, and take an active role in shaping policy—and to the IPR community for making the conversation so lively!
authors: Institute for Policy Research at Northwestern University

content_text:
How do journalists turn research into stories that shape policy and public understanding? 📰

Join Northwestern University Medill School and IPR experts Louise Kiernan, Natalie Moore, and Charles Whitaker on Monday for a discussion on bridging journalism and social science. Register here: https://lnkd.in/gVF9pD3n
authors: Institute for Policy Research at Northwestern University

content_text: How do officials use research to make tough public health and safety decisions? How can we bridge the gap between academia and policymaking? Join us on March 2 to hear local experts discuss how research is used (or not) in shaping public policy. Register: http://spr.ly/6041hvhdf
article_title: Evidence into Impact: Research at the Policy Table - Health Policy
authors: Institute for Policy Research at Northwestern University

content_text:
Men tend to develop heart disease earlier than women, according to new research by Northwestern University - The Feinberg School of Medicine's Sadiya Khan and colleagues. She told CNN that everyone should track their cholesterol, blood pressure, and blood sugar to stay on top of their heart health. "Know where your risk factors are, you know where your risk is, and then you can act on it," she said.
http://spr.ly/6049hqI3h
authors: Institute for Policy Research at Northwestern University

content_text:
After decades of progress, bias against gay people in the U.S. is rising—and fastest among young adults. In an op-ed for The New York Times, IPR's Eli Finkel and Tessa Charlesworth share new research on this alarming reversal and what might be driving it.

"Gay and lesbian people, newly woven into the fabric of mainstream society, may have been collateral damage in a broader revolt against a system that felt broken, especially among younger generations grappling most intensely with uncertainty about their future," they wrote.
http://spr.ly/6047hqo8A
article_title: Opinion | Americans Are Turning Against Gay People
authors: Institute for Policy Research at Northwestern University
